en.extract:
The little shrikethrush or little shrike-thrush (Colluricincla megarhyncha) is a species of passerine bird in the family Pachycephalidae that is found in New Guinea as well as in north and east Australia.


Taxonomy
The little shrikethrush was formally described and illustrated in 1832 as Muscicapa megarhyncha by the French naturalists Jean Quoy and Joseph Gaimard based on a specimen collected near Manokwari in the Bird's Head Peninsula of New Guinea. The specific epithet combines the Ancient Greek μεγα/mega meaning "much" with ῥυγχος/rhunkhos meaning "bill". The little shrikethrush is now placed in the genus Colluricincla that was introduced in 1827 by the naturalists Nicholas Vigors and Thomas Horsfield.
Molecular genetic studies of New Guinea populations of the little shrikethrush indicate high levels of genetic divergence, suggesting it may comprise more than one species. With at least eight separate clades identified as potential species splits, further research may lead to the re-classification of several new species. Alternate names for the little shrikethrush include the brown shrike-flycatcher and rufous shrike-thrush.
Sixteen subspecies are recognised:

C. m. megarhyncha (Quoy, JRC & Gaimard, JP, 1832) – Salawati Island (west Papuan islands), Bird's Head Peninsula to head of Cenderawasih Bay and adjacent southwestern New Guinea plain
C. m. parvula Gould, J, 1845 – northwestern Australia (Kimberley region, northern Western Australia, to Calvert River, eastern Northern Territory)
C. m. aruensis (Gray, GR, 1858) – Aru Islands (New Guinea)
C. m. fortis (Gadow, HF, 1883) – D'Entrecasteaux Archipelago (off southeastern New Guinea)
C. m. trobriandi (Hartert, EJO, 1896) – Trobriand Islands (off southeastern New Guinea)
C. m. despecta (Rothschild, LW & Hartert, EJO, 1903) – Papuan Peninsula, westward in the north to the Huon Gulf and in the south to the upper Fly-Digul Rivers
C. m. affinis (Gray, GR, 1862) – Waigeo (Raja Ampat Islands, off western New Guinea)
C. m. obscura (Meyer, AB, 1874) – Yapen (Cenderawasih Bay, off northwestern New Guinea)
C. m. hybridus (Meise, W, 1929) – northwestern and west-central New Guinea
C. m. discolor De Vis, CW, 1890 – Tagula Island (Louisiade Archipelago, off southeastern New Guinea)
C. m. tappenbecki Reichenow, A, 1899 – northeastern New Guinea (Astrolabe Bay to lower Sepik River)
C. m. madaraszi (Rothschild, LW & Hartert, EJO, 1903) – northeastern New Guinea (Huon Peninsula)
C. m. maeandrina (Stresemann, EFT, 1921) – northeastern New Guinea (upper Sepik River and Victor Emanuel Mountains)
C. m. rufogaster Gould, J, 1845 – eastern Australia (Bowen, northeastern Queensland, southward to Port Macquarie, central New South Wales)
C. m. normani (Mathews, GM, 1914) – southern New Guinea and northeastern Australia (western Torres Strait islands, northern Queensland from Nicholson River eastward to Normanby River catchment, Cape York Peninsula, northern Queensland)
C. m. griseata (Gray, GR, 1858) – northeastern Australia (Starke to Cape Bowling Green, northeastern Queensland)
The name Arafura shrikethrush was proposed in 2018 for the subspecies megarhyncha, batantae and parvula.


Distribution and habitat
It is found in Australia and New Guinea. Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests and subtropical or tropical moist montane forests.


Behaviour and ecology
During a study of toxicity in birds, two specimens of this species were tested. One of these specimens contained traces of batrachotoxins (BTXs) similar to those found in the secretions of Central and South American poison dart frogs.


References


External links

The '''little shrikethrush''' or '''little shrike-thrush''' ('''''Colluricincla megarhyncha''''') is a species of [[passerine]] bird in the family [[Pachycephalidae]] that is found in [[New Guinea]] as well as in north and east Australia.

==Taxonomy==
The little shrikethrush was [[species description|formally described]] and illustrated in 1832 as ''Muscicapa megarhyncha'' by the French naturalists [[Jean René Constant Quoy|Jean Quoy]] and [[Joseph Paul Gaimard|Joseph Gaimard]] based on a specimen collected near [[Manokwari]] in the [[Bird's Head Peninsula]] of [[New Guinea]].<ref>{{ cite book | last1=Quoy | first1=J.R.C. | author1-link=Jean René Constant Quoy | last2=Gaimard | first2=J.P. | author2-link=Joseph Paul Gaimard |name-list-style=amp | date=1832 | title=Voyage de la corvette l'Astrolabe: exécuté par ordre du roi, pendant les années 1826-1827-1828-1829: Zoologie | volume=1 | location=Paris | publisher=J. Tastu | pages=172-173, [https://digitalcollections.nypl.org/search/index?q=457faaf0-c5ff-012f-5ef6-58d385a7bc34 Plate 3 fig. 1] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/2064314 }} For the publication date see: {{ cite journal | last=Mlíkovský | first=Jiří  | year=2012 | title=The dating of the ornithological part of Quoy and Gaimard's ''Voyage de l'Astrolabe''| journal=Zoological Bibliography | volume=2 | issue=2&3 | pages=59-69 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/54038142 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Paynter | editor-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1986 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=12 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | location=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=37 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14482154 }}</ref> The specific epithet combines the [[Ancient Greek]] μεγα/''mega'' meaning "much" with ῥυγχος/''rhunkhos'' meaning "bill".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=megarhyncha | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=megarhyncha | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=12 December 2025}}</ref> The little shrikethrush is now placed in the genus ''[[Colluricincla]]'' that was introduced in 1827 by the naturalists [[Nicholas Aylward Vigors|Nicholas Vigors]] and [[Thomas Horsfield]].<ref name=avilist>{{ cite web | author=AviList Core Team | date=2025 | title=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025 | doi=10.2173/avilist.v2025 | doi-access=free | url=http://www.avilist.org/checklist/v2025/ | access-date=12 December 2025 }}</ref>

Molecular genetic studies of [[New Guinea]] populations of the little shrikethrush indicate high levels of genetic divergence, suggesting it may comprise more than one species.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Deiner | first1=K. | last2=Lemmon | first2=A.R. | last3=Mack | first3=A.L. | last4=Fleischer | first4=R.C. | last5=Dumbacher | first5=J.P. |name-list-style=amp | date=2011 | title=A passerine bird's evolution corroborates the geologic history of the island of New Guinea | journal=PLOS One | volume=6 | issue=5 | article-number=e19479 | doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0019479 | doi-access=free | pmc=3089620 }}</ref><ref name=marki>{{Cite journal | last1=Marki | first1=P.Z. | last2=Fjeldså | first2=J. | last3=Irestedt | first3=M. | last4=Jønsson | first4=K.A. |name-list-style=amp | date=2018 | title=Molecular phylogenetics and species limits in a cryptically coloured radiation of Australo-Papuan passerine birds (Pachycephalidae: ''Colluricincla'') | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | volume=124 | pages=100-105 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2018.02.029}}</ref> With at least eight separate [[clade]]s identified as potential species splits, further research may lead to the re-classification of several new species. Alternate names for the little shrikethrush include the brown shrike-flycatcher and rufous shrike-thrush.<ref name=hbw>{{ cite book | last=Boles | first=W.E. | year=2007 | chapter=Family Pachycephalidae (Whistlers) | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Sargatal | editor3-first=J . | title=Handbook of the Birds of the World | volume=12: Picathartes to Tits and Chickadees | location=Barcelona, Spain | publisher=Lynx Edicions | isbn=978-84-96553-42-2 | pages=374–437 [431-432 | chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofbirdso0012unse/page/431/mode/1up | chapter-url-access=registration }}</ref>

Sixteen [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=avilist/>
* ''C. m. megarhyncha'' ([[Jean René Constant Quoy|Quoy, JRC]] & [[Joseph Paul Gaimard|Gaimard, JP]], 1832) – Salawati Island (west Papuan islands), [[Bird's Head Peninsula]] to head of [[Cenderawasih Bay]] and adjacent southwestern [[New Guinea]] plain
* ''C. m. parvula'' [[John Gould|Gould, J]], 1845 – northwestern Australia ([[Kimberley (Western Australia)|Kimberley]] region, northern [[Western Australia]], to Calvert River, eastern [[Northern Territory]])
* ''C. m. aruensis'' ([[George Robert Gray|Gray, GR]], 1858) – [[Aru Islands Regency|Aru Islands]] ([[New Guinea]])
* ''C. m. fortis'' ([[Hans Friedrich Gadow|Gadow, HF]], 1883) – [[D'Entrecasteaux Islands|D'Entrecasteaux Archipelago]] (off southeastern [[New Guinea]])
* ''C. m. trobriandi'' ([[Ernst Hartert|Hartert, EJO]], 1896) – [[Trobriand Islands]] (off southeastern [[New Guinea]])
* ''C. m. despecta'' ([[Walter Rothschild, 2nd Baron Rothschild|Rothschild, LW]] & Hartert, EJO, 1903) – Papuan Peninsula, westward in the north to the [[Huon Gulf]] and in the south to the upper Fly-Digul Rivers
* ''C. m. affinis'' (Gray, GR, 1862) – [[Waigeo]] ([[Raja Ampat Islands]], off western [[New Guinea]])
* ''C. m. obscura'' ([[Adolf Bernhard Meyer|Meyer, AB]], 1874) – [[Yapen]] ([[Cenderawasih Bay]], off northwestern [[New Guinea]])
* ''C. m. hybridus'' ([[Wilhelm Meise|Meise, W]], 1929) – northwestern and west-central [[New Guinea]]
* ''C. m. discolor'' [[Charles Walter De Vis|De Vis, CW]], 1890 – [[Tagula Island]] ([[Louisiade Archipelago]], off southeastern [[New Guinea]])
* ''C. m. tappenbecki'' [[Anton Reichenow|Reichenow, A]], 1899 – northeastern [[New Guinea]] (Astrolabe Bay to lower Sepik River)
* ''C. m. madaraszi'' (Rothschild, LW & Hartert, EJO, 1903) – northeastern [[New Guinea]] ([[Huon Peninsula]])
* ''C. m. maeandrina'' ([[Erwin Stresemann|Stresemann, EFT]], 1921) – northeastern [[New Guinea]] (upper Sepik River and Victor Emanuel Mountains)
* ''C. m. rufogaster'' Gould, J, 1845 – eastern Australia (Bowen, northeastern [[Queensland]], southward to Port Macquarie, central [[New South Wales]])
* ''C. m. normani'' ([[Gregory Mathews|Mathews, GM]], 1914) – southern [[New Guinea]] and northeastern Australia (western [[Torres Strait]] islands, northern [[Queensland]] from Nicholson River eastward to Normanby River catchment, [[Cape York Peninsula]], northern [[Queensland]])
* ''C. m. griseata'' (Gray, GR, 1858) – northeastern Australia (Starke to Cape Bowling Green, northeastern [[Queensland]])

The name Arafura shrikethrush was proposed in 2018 for the subspecies ''megarhyncha'', ''batantae'' and ''parvula''.<ref name=marki/>

==Distribution and habitat==
It is found in Australia and New Guinea. Its natural [[habitat]]s are subtropical or tropical moist lowland [[forest]]s and subtropical or tropical moist [[montane forest]]s.<ref name=IUCN/>

==Behaviour and ecology==
During a study of [[toxicity]] in birds, two specimens of this species were tested. One of these specimens contained traces of [[batrachotoxins]] (BTXs) similar to those found in the secretions of Central and South American [[poison dart frog]]s.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Dumbacher |first1=J. P. |last2=Spande |first2=T. F. |last3=Daly |first3=J. W. |name-list-style=amp |title=Batrachotoxin alkaloids from passerine birds: A second toxic bird genus (''Ifrita kowaldi'') from New Guinea |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |date=2000 |volume=97 |issue=24 |pages=12970–12975 |doi=10.1073/pnas.200346897 |pmid=11035772 |pmc=27162 }}</ref>
